item.title: Catholic nuns sue New York, say assisted suicide law forces them to violate their faith
item.description: Catholic nuns who have cared for 45,000 dying patients sue New York, claiming the Medical Aid in Dying Act forces them to facilitate assisted suicide.
item.content:encoded:
Catholic nuns and faith-based healthcare providers who have spent generations caring for New York's dying are suing the state, arguing its new assisted suicide law will force them to violate their religious beliefs by participating in a practice they consider morally wrong.

The lawsuit, filed Friday in federal court by over 10 Catholic plaintiffs, including the Dominican Sisters of Hawthorne, the Carmelite Sisters for the Aged and Infirm, the Little Sisters of the Poor, the Diocese of Rockville Centre and Catholic Health, argues New York's Medical Aid in Dying Act compels religious healthcare providers to counsel terminally ill patients about physician-assisted suicide, make referrals to participating providers and, in some cases, accommodate assisted suicide in their facilities.

The plaintiffs say those requirements violate the First Amendment, federal healthcare protections and longstanding Catholic teaching on the sanctity of human life.

REP CLAUDIA TENNEY: WHY IS NEW YORK PUNISHING NUNS WHO CARE FOR THE DYING?

"New York has put the Sisters to a stark choice: either abandon their religious beliefs regarding the sanctity of life or face significant fines and penalties," the lawsuit reads. "Through its euphemistically-named Medical Aid in Dying Act, which builds upon requirements in the Palliative Care Information Act, New York has conscripted even religious healthcare providers and institutions to participate in the provision of physician-assisted suicide."

New York’s Medical Aid in Dying Act, which was signed into law in February, lets those who are 18 years and older who have been diagnosed with a terminal illness and given six months or less to live to request and self-administer life-ending medication if they are experiencing immense suffering, according to Compassion and Choices, who supports the new law.

The group notes the measure is based on Oregon's long-standing assisted dying law.

HOSPICE CEO ASKS CONGRESS HOW A PROVIDER CAN OPERATE 'OUT OF A BURRITO STAND IN CALIFORNIA' WITH NO OVERSIGHT

According to the complaint, the law could compel religious organizations to facilitate physician-assisted suicide by giving patients information about the practice, permitting staff to discuss it with terminally ill patients, and helping patients complete steps required to qualify for life-ending medication.

The plaintiffs also argue the law would require referrals to willing providers and prevent the organizations from disciplining employees who provide such counseling or assistance in conflict with the institutions' religious beliefs.

Rosary Hill Home, run by the Dominican Sisters of Hawthorne, one of the plaintiffs, has cared for roughly 45,000 low-income cancer patients in New York, providing free end-of-life care regardless of their faith or cultural background.

SUPREME COURT UNANIMOUSLY SLAPS DOWN BLUE STATE TARGETING PRO-LIFE GROUP

Mother Marie Edward, O.P., Superior General of the Dominican Sisters of Hawthorne, said, "For over 125 years, we have ministered to the poor who are dying of cancer as if they were Christ Himself. Our calling is to offer comfort, prayer, and loving medical care to those in their final days—not death."

Edward added, "We pray the court will protect our freedom to remain faithful to that ministry God has entrusted to us."

In April, the Dominican Sisters of Hawthorne sued New York Gov. Kathy Hochul, alleging the state’s "Lesbian, gay, bisexual and transgender, and people living with HIV long-term care facility residents' bill of rights" could force them to choose between their faith and their mission or face fines, loss of licensing and even jail time.

Mother Mary Rose Heery, O. Carm., prioress general of the Carmelite Sisters for the Aged and Infirm, said in a statement, "At our homes, we bring Christ’s unbounded love to elderly New Yorkers of every background and walk of life."

Heery added, "We strive to bring His compassion to those entrusted to our care, ensuring that no resident ever has to die alone. This law strikes at the heart of that vocation."

Mark Rienzi, president of Becket, who is representing the Carmelite Sisters, the Dominican Sisters, the Benedictine Sisters, and the Little Sisters of the Poor, as well as Bishop John O. Barres and the Diocese of Rockville Centre, said in a statement that, "New York is forcing sick and dying people to consider suicide at their lowest moment, regardless of whether they ask for it or not."

"Worse, it’s conscripting the nuns who care for them into its suicide cult on pain of crushing fines and professional ruin," Rienzi added. "That is both unconscionable and unconstitutional. We’re confident the federal courts will put a stop to it."

Martin Nussbaum, general counsel for Catholic Benefits Association, who is representing the Dominican Sisters of Hawthorne in the transgender bill of rights lawsuit, told Fox News Digital that the Catholic Benefits Association "is entirely supportive of the Becket lawsuit seeking religious exemption from New York’s assisted suicide law."

"Just as in the Catholic Benefits Association case for the Dominican Sisters of Hawthorne, the State of New York is trampling on the religious liberty and speech rights of Catholic nuns providing selfless service to men and women on their final journey," Nussbaum added.

Fox News Digital reached out to Hochul, New York Attorney General Letitia James, the Carmelite Sisters for the Aged and Infirm, the Little Sisters of the Poor, and the Diocese of Rockville Centre and Catholic Health for comment.

item.title: 'Long Island Lolita' Amy Fisher's son charged as cops probe possible microwave link in cat's killing
item.description: Brett Bellera, son of Amy Fisher, and his girlfriend face felony animal cruelty charges after their cat allegedly died from extreme heat exposure.
item.content:encoded:
The adult son of notorious "Long Island Lolita" Amy Fisher and his girlfriend are facing felony animal-cruelty charges in the disturbing death of their pet cat, Reaper, according to Florida court records.

Brett Anthony Bellera, 25, and Jennifer Armenti, 36, were charged in separate companion cases with aggravated cruelty to animals after investigators concluded the Brown-and-White tabby died from "involuntary heat exposure."

Microwave radiation was among the potential causes identified by a forensic veterinarian. The other possibilities were immersion in extremely hot water or exposure to hot-water vapor, according to probable-cause affidavits obtained by Fox News Digital.

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE

The mystery began on Dec. 15, 2024, when a friend of the couple alerted Broward County Animal Care that Reaper had died under potentially unnatural circumstances.

Armenti allegedly told investigators she had awakened that morning and found the cat dead on the bathroom floor. By the time animal-care employees arrived, however, Reaper’s body was outside on the apartment’s back patio.

FOLLOW THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM ON X

Investigators found the cat inside a plastic laundry basket, saying that the cat was "stiff" and positioned unnaturally, with a large amount of blood pooled beneath the body.

Bellera allegedly told an officer that he and Armenti had gone to bed the previous night and discovered the cat dead the following morning. He suggested Reaper may have accidentally consumed cleaning products, according to his affidavit.

KILLER UNDONE BY DECADES-OLD THREADS — GLOVE, TOWEL AND SHIRT STITCH TOGETHER COLD CASE JUSTICE

But a forensic necropsy conducted through the American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals pointed investigators in a far darker direction.

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST TRUE CRIME NEWS

A licensed veterinarian concluded that Reaper suffered fatal injuries caused by extreme heat. Microscopic tissue analysis allegedly indicated the exposure did not kill the cat immediately, meaning Reaper endured severe pain before dying, the affidavits say.

SEND US A TIP

Bellera’s case was filed on June 3, 2026, and records show he was arrested the following day. Armenti was arrested later that month. Each posted a $25,000 bond, and both dockets reference electronic-monitoring or house-arrest agreements. Both have pleaded not guilty, and their cases remain active.

GOT A TIP?

Bellera is Fisher’s eldest son with her former husband, retired NYPD officer Louis Bellera.

Fisher became one of the country’s most infamous tabloid figures after she shot Mary Jo Buttafuoco, the wife of her adult lover, outside the family’s Long Island, New York, home in 1992. Fisher, who was just 16 years old, was sent to prison for seven years for shooting Buttafuoco.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

Dubbed the "Long Island Lolita," Fisher first met Joey Buttafuoco when her father brought his car into the 35-year-old's auto shop.

By 1992, Fisher had grown jealous of Buttafuoco's wife, Mary Jo, and took it upon herself to eliminate her. Fisher went to Buttafuoco's home, pretending to be her sister, and shot Mary Jo in the head in broad daylight.

GET BREAKING NEWS BY EMAIL

Mary Jo survived, but she still has the bullet in her head.

There were numerous made-for-TV movies produced about the scandalous affair. One film even starred actress Drew Barrymore.

After serving seven years, Fisher was released and used her infamy to enter the world of adult entertainment. Fox News Digital has reached out to Bellera and Armenti's attorneys for comment.

item.title: Reporter's Notebook: The Republican-led vote that revealed Democrats' new divide on Israel
item.description: A House vote to cut $3 billion in aid to Israel split Democrats nearly down the middle, with 103 voting to slash assistance to the Israeli military.
item.content:encoded:
Things sometimes evolve slowly in politics. There’s a perception, axiom or maxim which is agreed upon in politics. But over time, it changes.

Sometimes the change is subterranean. The tectonic political plates move. But no one is quite aware of what’s happening until one event crystallizes the shift.

Such is the case with support in the Democratic Party for Israel.

HOUSE DEMOCRATS FRACTURE BADLY OVER MASSIE AMENDMENT TO CUT $3.3B IN U.S. AID TO ISRAEL

The politics of supporting Israel have moved for several years now. Especially after Israel’s response to the deadly attacks by Hamas and pro-Palestinian groups on October 7, 2023.

But then there’s that one moment which seems to change everything.

That was certainly on display in the House of Representatives during a single roll call vote last week.

Rep. Thomas Massie, R-Ky., engineered an amendment to a State Department funding bill to cut off $3 billion in aid to Israel. Some Democrats viewed this as a political effort to cleave the party.

Whether it was political or not remains to be seen. But Massie’s amendment split Democrats nearly down the middle.

The House defeated Massie’s effort, 314 to 104. Massie was the only Republican who voted in favor of slashing aid to Israel. But 103 Democrats joined Massie to also suspend the assistance. Ten Democrats voted "present," only 98 Democrats voted to reject Massie’s effort.

The Democratic divide over Israel has widened for a while now. It used to be rare to have more than a few Democrats who wouldn’t stand beside Israel. But that House roll call vote truly underscored where the party is now. It exposed deepening internal fissures.

"This is an important moment because nothing will be the same on this issue ever again," observed Rep. Greg Casar, D-Texas, chair of the House Progressive Caucus. "It used to be that just a small number of House Democrats would vote against sending taxpayer dollars and weapons for the Israeli military. Then, two years ago, 37 House Democrats voted to not send billions of dollars to the Israeli military."

Casar added that this was "not the end. This is a new beginning" for Democrats.

"Democrats here in the House have finally decided to do what the people, our voters, the constituents we represent, have been asking us to do," said Rep. Ilhan Omar, D-Minn. "I am proud that they have finally decided to lead with their morals."

The left says the party is simply reflecting where its core voters stand.

NEW YORK TIMES RAISES EYEBROWS BY REFERRING TO AIPAC AS 'HARD-RIGHT' PRO-ISRAEL GROUP

"You're seeing a shift. The American people are growing weary of this war and what's happening," said Rep. Julie Johnson, D-Texas.

Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., argued the vote wasn’t "a litmus test" for the party. But said the vote addressed another phenomena.

"Americans of all backgrounds are deeply frustrated that our needs are not being met at home. But our resources are being sent abroad in a way that is not representative of our values and not representative of who we are," said Ocasio-Cortez.

Former House Majority Leader Steny Hoyer, D-Md., is retiring from his seat early next year. Few lawmakers from either party have been as vocal as Hoyer in supporting Israel over the past four decades.

"This amendment would embolden the enemies of peace, those pursuing the complete elimination of Israel and those who seek the death of Jews," said Hoyer. "It's a vote against American security."

Hoyer stepped back from the House Democratic leadership in early 2023. But today’s Democratic leaders concede the party currently views Israel through a different lens.

"It's not lost on me that the actions the (Israeli) Prime Minister has taken has not made it easier for us to be supporters of a strong U.S.-Israel relationship," said House Democratic Caucus Chairman Pete Aguilar, D-CA.

Even Democrats who voted in favor of the aid say there are shifts in Israel which prompted Democrats to recalibrate their views.

"I think a lot of people are concerned about Netanyahu and are concerned about some of the activities over the past couple of years," said Rep. Tom Suozzi, D-N.Y.

Rep. Wesley Bell, D-Mo., voted against cutting off money for Israel. Bell is a freshman who defeated progressive former Rep. Cori Bush, D-Mo., in the primary two years ago. He then won the general election that fall. Bush is a skeptic of Israel. She is challenging Bell in next month’s primary.

"It's important that we do stand with our allies because our sworn enemies are not going away," said Bell.

It’s unclear if Bell’s vote – pitted against Bush’s more progressive stance – could dictate who wins the primary. But it’s worth watching.

Pro-Israel Republicans worry about the anti-Israel tide shift on the Democratic side of the aisle.

"I think they've been captured by crazies. You can see it in their elections by people who hate Jews." Said Rep. Randy Fine, R-Fla.

But the new positioning by the Democratic party concerns Sen. John Fetterman, D-Penn., He’s a staunch ally of Isreal. Fetterman is threatening to desert Democrats if the party deserts Israel.

"That's my red line in the Democratic party," said Fetterman. "Look at the people that are winning these primaries right now. They're not just ‘I'm just pro-Palestinian. I am deeply anti-anti-Israel.’"

SEN JOHN FETTERMAN PLEDGES TO BE 'LAST MAN STANDING' IN THE DEMOCRATIC PARTY IN SUPPORT OF ISRAEL

Notably Fetterman did not say that he would necessarily become a Republican. But Democrats need Fetterman in their fold.

Republicans hold a 53-45 advantage over the Democrats. Sens. Bernie Sanders, I-Vt., and Angus King, I-Maine, caucus with the Democrats. That gets you to 47 senators who align with the Democrats. So Democrats need a net win of four seats to seize control of the Senate next year. Democrats hope to hold Michigan, Georgia and New Hampshire. But they are also looking to flip seats in Maine, North Carolina, Ohio, Texas, Iowa and Alaska. Any combination of four seats scores Democrats the majority.

But let’s say Democrats do collect four seats and earn an effective 51-49 advantage. Fetterman could make the Senate 50-50 if he bolts from the Democrats. The Senate has historically worked out a power-sharing arrangement during other 50-50 breakdowns. But regardless, Republicans would have nominal control thanks to the tie-breaking vote of Vice President JD Vance, who is President of the Senate.

The Democrats need Fetterman more than he needs them right now.

And as for Israel? Israel needs Democrats more than they need Israel.

Congressional Republicans still support Israel for now. But if Democrats win control of the House - and perhaps the Senate - support for Israel will look very different next year thanks to the Democrats’ morphing politics.

That’s all evolved for some time now. But you can point to last week’s vote as the seminal moment when it all changed for the Democratic party.

And for Israel.

item.title: Missouri's Eli Drinkwitz becomes latest SEC coach to make ridiculous complaints about Big Ten schedules
item.description: Eli Drinkwitz blames Big Ten non-conference schedules for SEC struggles, but Missouri's 2025 slate included six straight home games to open the season.
item.content:encoded:
The more that college football changes, the more some things remain the same. In this case, SEC coaches complaining about schedules.

As we rapidly approach the start of the 2026 season, the landscape of the sport has changed significantly. In the pre-NIL era, the SEC, led by Nick Saban's Alabama dynasty, dominated college football. In 10 years, from 2013-2022, SEC teams won six National Championships, three for the Crimson Tide, two for Georgia, and the Joe Burrow-led 2019 LSU Tigers.

But NIL almost immediately upended roster construction, creating more parity within the sport as former, say, Alabama backups, took bigger paydays to start elsewhere. As a result, there's a much more level playing field across college football, other teams in other conferences have improved their rosters, and the top of the SEC is no longer heads and shoulders above the competition.

JOEL KLATT ROASTS SEC: NICK SABAN ISN'T THERE 'WITH HIS BIG OLD COATTAILS FOR YOU TO RIDE AS A CONFERENCE'

Unsurprisingly then, the SEC has failed to even reach the National Championship game in three straight seasons. And even worse, teams from the Big Ten Conference have not only reached the final, but won it in each of those years. Based on some preseason analytics-driven rankings, the Big Ten might be favored to win its fourth straight title with Ohio State and Oregon topping the sport.

There are reasonable, logical explanations for this abrupt change in power structures. And then there's what Missouri head coach Eli Drinkwitz says. At SEC media days, when describing why the Big Ten has surpassed his conference, Drinkwitz said it comes down to "When you don't play a (non-conference) A4 game," per Brett McMurphy.

Brandon Marcello from CBS Sports added that Drinkwitz believes the SEC coaches already "regret moving to a nine-game conference schedule," and that "Lane Kiffin has long been in front saying a move from eight games would be an issue."

Ah. So that's the problem. The Big Ten hasn't had enough tough non-conference games. Well, let's take a look at Missouri's non-conference schedule in 2025 under Drinkwitz, shall we?

LANE KIFFIN MAKES RIDICULOUS EXCUSES FOR SEC'S POOR RECENT PERFORMANCE IN COLLEGE FOOTBALL PLAYOFF

Real murderers' row of tough teams, isn't it? Even better, Drinkwitz and Missouri's schedule was so hard they played, and this is not a misprint, six straight home games to start the season. They opened the season on Aug. 28, and their first road game of the year, and again, this is not a misprint, was Oct. 18.

They played four road games, at Auburn, Vanderbilt, Oklahoma, and Arkansas. They went 2-2. Their total distance traveled from Columbia all season, was 1,805 miles. Big Ten Conference team USC played a road non-conference game against Notre Dame, which is nearly 2,100 miles from Los Angeles. One Big Ten team traveled more for a single game than Missouri did all season. "And that stuff matters," as Kiffin said when making farcical, inaccurate excuses for the SEC's recent underperformance.

It is remarkable, dishonest deflection. Yes, Missouri still played a tough schedule because of their conference games, but to act as though the Big Ten only won a National Championship because of non-conference schedules is ludicrous. Indiana finished its undefeated season by beating Ohio State, Alabama, Oregon and Miami. Missouri lost to Virginia, 13-7, in the TaxSlayer Gator Bowl. Apparently that's due to the much tougher non-conference slate Missouri faced and their grueling travel schedule of four road games where they quite literally never left the central time zone. It's almost impossible to give yourself more small advantages than Missouri did.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

All of these excuses and explanations and justifications are wrong, and missing the point. The Big Ten has caught up to the SEC because of NIL and the transfer portal. The SEC's depth has improved because they distribute tons of money to each program, they have large fanbases that support the football teams, and there are fewer incentives to sit at Alabama now than there were before. Games between elite teams are mostly 55%/45% or 60%/40% toss-ups, and contending programs like Texas or Georgia could rebound and win a title this year.

The SEC has long had non-conference scheduling advantages, with teams like Missouri giving themselves four free wins, eight home games, FCS bye weeks in November and little travel. Then pointing to win-loss records, propped up by four free wins, as "proof" of how tough the SEC is. Now that other conferences are emulating them, they're crying foul. Yes, moving to nine conference games is going to hurt their records. That's what happens when advantages are removed.

It's impossible to take these scheduling complaints seriously when the SEC is 2-7 against other Power 4 conference teams in the College Football Playoff the past three seasons. Even including games against themselves, the SEC is just 5-9 the past three tournaments. The Big Ten is 13-5. Maybe worry about that first.

item.title: Bryson DeChambeau wanted President Trump to intervene in his Open Championship penalty: report
item.description: Bryson DeChambeau reportedly wanted President Trump to intervene in his two-stroke penalty at the Open Championship, but the president denied the request.
item.content:encoded:
Bryson DeChambeau’s two-stroke penalty at the 2026 Open Championship was one of the most talked-about incidents on the course throughout the tournament, and it continues to make headlines after a recent report mentioned he wanted to get President Donald Trump involved.

DeChambeau, who shares a close friendship with Trump, especially after playing golf together on the LIV Golf star’s YouTube channel, was docked two strokes during his second round after he was seen stomping on tall grass near his ball, which is an illegal move.

Rory McIlroy claimed that DeChambeau was holding the final major of the golf season hostage after refusing to sign his second-round scorecard after finishing.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Now, golf writer Geoff Shackelford is reporting DeChambeau wanted to get Trump involved.

"Sources with knowledge of the thirty-minute discussion said DeChambeau wanted the President to join a campaign against the eventual two-stroke penalty that ultimately dropped his score from 66 to 68," Shackelford wrote. "The request came after DeChambeau had already been given a rare bit of special dispensation to return to the scene where he’s stomped around tall fescue rough in his backswing."

GOLF ANALYST BRANDEL CHAMBLEE DISGUSTINGLY BURIES BRYSON DECHAMBEAU AFTER BRITISH OPEN PENALTY

Trump reportedly denied DeChambeau’s request to step in. The White House has not immediately responded to a request for comment by Fox News Digital.

There’s no telling what DeChambeau may have thought Trump could do, but after what occurred in the FIFA World Cup with Team USA, perhaps he was hoping for a similar result.

Trump contacted FIFA president Gianni Infantino, yet another close friend, to ask about a review of the red card given to Folarin Balogun – Team USA’s star striker throughout the tournament. Balogun’s controversial red card would’ve had him sidelined for the Round of 16 against Belgium, but FIFA suspended the red card, allowing him to play.

It ultimately made no difference, as Belgium handed the U.S. a 4-1 defeat to knock the host country out of the tournament earlier this month.

DeChambeau finished tied for 14th place at 4-under for the tournament at Royal Birkdale this past weekend. New Zealand’s Ryan Fox secured his first-ever major championship after birdying his final hole to beat out American Cameron Young by one stroke with a 10-under tournament finish.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

item.title: EXCLUSIVE: Ousted DHS counterterrorism chief sues millionaire ex over alleged sugar daddy smear campaign
item.description: A former DHS counterterrorism official filed a defamation lawsuit accusing her ex-boyfriend of leaking a fabricated sugar daddy scandal to end her career.
item.content:encoded:
FIRST ON FOX: A former high-ranking Department of Homeland Security (DHS) official who was publicly sidelined in a "sugar daddy" media firestorm is fighting back with a major defamation lawsuit, accusing her wealthy ex-boyfriend of weaponizing her gender to destroy her counterterrorism career after she rejected a marriage ultimatum.

Julia Varvaro, the 29-year-old former DHS deputy assistant secretary for counterterrorism, filed a federal complaint Monday in the Eastern District of Virginia, alleging defamation per se and defamation by implication against Robert "Bob" Bianchi, multimillionaire CEO of federal contractor SDVO Solutions.

The lawsuit accuses Bianchi, 57, of fabricating a DHS Office of Inspector General (OIG) complaint that falsely claimed Varvaro’s college education was funded by "sugar daddies" and her jewelry consisted of "trophies."

He then leaked the confidential complaint, private photos and text messages to The Daily Mail to ignite a tabloid scandal, according to the suit.

NAVY VETERAN WHO BEAT CNN IN COURT SAYS CIA WANTS HIM TO DELETE, BURN BOOK MANUSCRIPT: ‘THAT'S CENSORSHIP’

The lawsuit specifically targets "defamation by implication," accusing Bianchi of strategically manipulating intimate text messages to frame their age-gap relationship as a transactional "sugar baby" arrangement.

Attorneys argue a leaked text message exchange where Bianchi teased, "Don’t f— with Daddy on fashion," and Varvaro replied, "Daddy knows all," was lighthearted banter about their age difference — not evidence of sex work.

The lawsuit also addresses Apple Cash transfers that Bianchi framed as "consideration for sex," noting the payments were standard financial flows of a committed relationship.

In one private text exchange, when Bianchi suffered from kidney stones in Europe and Varvaro allegedly covered his emergency medical bills, screenshots included in the suit show he texted her, "I'm sorry lover ... Thanks for your help today. I was struggling," to which Varvaro replied, "It's okay lover as long as you are good is the only thing that matters ... Whatever you need I will figure out as long as you're good."

BLAKE LIVELY ALLEGES ‘MEAN GIRL’ SMEAR CAMPAIGN DAMAGED HER CAREER, SEEKS UP TO $296M

Later, after she drove him through snowy roads, screenshots show Bianchi messaged her: "I love you Julia. Thanks for being You," and gave her his home keycode, texting, "I’m yours lover... you are always welcome, any time or day."

Bianchi also frequently messaged her support, writing, "I'm proud of the work you do. You're making a difference," and expressed his gratitude after introducing her to his children: "Thanks for a nice evening. I appreciate you meeting all my kids... I love you."

However, attorneys claim the tone shifted during a historic 76-day government shutdown in February, when Varvaro was furloughed.

The lawsuit alleges that when she planned to take a temporary bartending job to pay rent, Bianchi insisted she come to him for the funds instead. But when she accepted, Bianchi used her financial vulnerability as leverage for control.

In another exchange, he purportedly texted her: "I told you I am not paying your bills until we are engaged."

Varvaro said she refused the ultimatum, ended the relationship, and told him she did not need "an ATM."

In an exclusive interview with Fox News Digital, Varvaro’s attorney, Vel Freedman, described the fallout of the subsequent media controversy as "devastating."

NY TIMES COLUMNIST KRISTOF SAYS NO PLANS TO SEEK OFFICE AGAIN AMID DISCLOSURE ISSUES, QUERIES ON UNUSED FUNDS

Freedman revealed Varvaro has been "terribly" impacted, suffering public humiliation, losing her position and seeking therapy under the care of physicians.

He also highlighted the unique weaponization of gender in the alleged smear campaign, noting that Bianchi's allegations were tailored to be "extraordinarily devastating to her career and her reputation... in a way that is sort of unique to being a woman."

The legal battle flips the narrative from April 22, when the media initially reported she was ousted. An anonymous complainant, now identified as Bianchi, told The Daily Mail he spent $40,000 on Varvaro, that she used drugs, and had a dating profile on Seeking — a website that markets itself as "a space where love and luxury meet."

Varvaro maintains she met Bianchi on Hinge, a separate dating app that uses a Nobel-Prize-winning algorithm to learn users' "type," according to the company.

Once his identity was unmasked by the press, however, attorneys said Bianchi recanted, telling People Magazine their relationship was actually "brief and personal" and "not a 'sugar' relationship."

Despite his retraction, Varvaro was terminated from DHS and had her employment offer as a CIA targeting officer rescinded.

It is unclear what specific concerns or infractions prompted Varvaro's dismissal.

The CIA, DHS, Hinge and Seeking did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital's requests for comment. Bianchi could not immediately be reached for comment.

item.title: Johnson has three-word answer on whether House will leave early
item.description: Mike Johnson vows House Republicans won't leave Washington until votes on the stock trading ban, stopgap spending, and defense bill are complete.
item.content:encoded:
With the House facing a jam-packed final week before August recess, House Speaker Mike Johnson, R-La., is making clear lawmakers won't leave Washington until they finish their work.

Asked by Fox News on Monday whether he would send the House home early if GOP leaders failed to win a critical vote slated for Tuesday afternoon that would tee up consideration of several major bills, Johnson had a quick three-word response.

"I will not," he said.

The speaker’s comments come as he attempts this week to put a GOP-authored stock trading ban on the floor along with a stopgap spending measure funding the government through the midterm elections, while bringing a must-pass annual defense policy bill up for a second time.

GOP'S $95B DEFENSE PACKAGE SURVIVES CRITICAL HURDLE AMID FISCAL HAWK REVOLT

GOP leaders are also racing to pass a $95 billion budget framework providing for $73 billion in defense and intelligence funding as the Iran war drags on while also funding agriculture and election integrity priorities. The Trump-endorsed blueprint has spurred criticism from fiscal conservatives, who argue it lacks spending offsets.

The first test comes Monday evening when the House Rules Committee is expected to report out a rule setting up a chamber-wide procedural vote on the legislative items Tuesday.

Given House Republicans’ slim margins, Johnson can afford to lose just three GOP defections if all members are present and voting.

House conservatives have repeatedly demonstrated a willingness to vote down party-line procedural votes this year over various concerns, including frustration over the SAVE America Act stalling in the Senate.

"The fact that there is no payfor and no language defunding Planned Parenthood is enough for me to vote no," Rep. Eric Burlison, R-Mo., a fiscal hawk, told Fox News Digital on Monday, regarding the budget framework.

Trump urged Republicans in a Truth Social post Monday to support the budget plan, arguing it would include as much of the election measure "as possible."

GOP CIVIL WAR ERUPTS OVER TRUMP'S LATEST AGENDA PUSH AS KEY REPUBLICANS THREATEN TO SINK MEGABILL

Burlison has voiced concern about getting elements of the SAVE America Act through the Senate’s stringent budget reconciliation requirements — a process in which the Senate parliamentarian plays an outsize role.

"I am open to a dialogue with the administration on how we can ensure that the SAVE language is solid and what we might be able to do with stopping abortions by mail," he told Fox News Digital.

Meanwhile, Sen. Rick Scott, R-Fla., said Monday that Congress should stay in Washington until a short-term funding package and the SAVE America Act reach Trump’s desk.

"After this week, the House and Senate won’t be in session at the same time again until SEPTEMBER 14 if we keep the current schedule," he wrote on social media. "That’s insane. We STILL need to pass the SAVE America Act and prevent Democrats from shutting down the government in October."

"This would NOT fly in the business world. We need to stay here until we get ALL our work done. That’s what the American people expect of us," he continued.

item.title: Birth tourism fight shifts to US ‘tropical maternity ward’ with history of Chinese exploitation
item.description: Rep. Morgan Griffith's bill targets birthright citizenship in four U.S. territories, citing Insular Cases precedent after the Supreme Court's ruling.
item.content:encoded:
The congressional fight over birthright citizenship shifted to the U.S. territories this week after the Supreme Court struck down President Donald Trump’s effort to deny citizenship to children born in the United States to parents who are unlawfully or temporarily present.

Rep. Morgan Griffith, R-Va., said a Senate proposal as well as the overall birthright citizenship debate leaves untouched the five inhabited U.S. territories, where citizenship at birth was established by federal law rather than directly under the Fourteenth Amendment.

"The ‘Barbara’ decision leaves open the door for Congressional action on citizenship in the American territories," Griffith said in introducing the End Birthright Citizenship for Territories Act, referring to the Supreme Court’s recent birthright citizenship ruling.

BIRTH TOURISM CRACKDOWN EXPANDS AS HOUSE CHAIRMAN RAISES CRIMINAL CONSPIRACY CASE

"Because of the current birthright laws in place in the territories, any child born in a U.S. territory minus American Samoa, regardless of a parent’s nationality, accesses U.S. citizenship automatically."

Four of the five U.S. territories – the U.S. Virgin Islands, Puerto Rico, Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands (CNMI) would be covered by Griffith’s bill. American Samoans are not automatically conferred U.S. citizenship upon birth.

"This bill should stop the so-called ‘birth tourism’ that is taking place in the American territories," Griffith said.

SEN ERIC SCHMITT SEEKS TO END BIRTH TOURISM, REVOKE CITIZENSHIP FOR BABIES OF FOREIGN DIPLOMATS

Congress established birthright citizenship in most territories by statute, and Griffith argues it therefore has the authority to revise those laws.

"Accordingly, I am leading the charge in Congress to rectify this problem, clarify U.S. citizenship laws in the territories and combat foreign nationals who are exploiting loopholes in our citizenship laws to take advantage of U.S. generosity."

After the federal government assumed control of CNMI immigration in 2009, DHS used parole authority to permit eligible Chinese and Russian visitors to enter the islands without visas — a policy critics say facilitated birth tourism.

'IT'S INSANE': GOP SENATOR SAYS SUPREME COURT BIRTHRIGHT RULING HANDS CHINA A CITIZENSHIP LOOPHOLE

That change led CNMI to be "flooded with so-called birth tourists" in what a New York Post report described as a "tropical maternity ward" of citizens ripe to reach voting age around 2030.

Residents of the five inhabited territories cannot vote in the presidential general election while living there, though they participate in party presidential nominating contests. Guam, the Virgin Islands, American Samoa and CNMI elect House delegates, while Puerto Rico elects a resident commissioner. None may vote on final passage of legislation on the House floor.

Federal prosecutors shut down one of the CNMI's largest commercial birth-tourism operations during the first Trump administration.

Prosecutors said one individual, Sen Sun, operated a lucrative birth-tourism business on Saipan that "illegally employed dozens of caretakers… all Chinese nationals… without work authorization," according to the U.S. Attorney’s Office for Guam and CNMI. Sun pleaded guilty to harboring illegal aliens.

Griffith’s bill relies partly on the Insular Cases, a group of early-20th-century Supreme Court decisions addressing how constitutional protections apply in U.S. territories.

Lower courts later applied that territorial framework in citizenship cases, including the D.C. Circuit’s 2015 decision in Tuaua v. United States, which held that the Citizenship Clause does not confer birthright citizenship on people born in American Samoa.

Asked about Griffith’s bill and the recent reports on birth tourism in her territory, Del. Kimberlyn King-Hinds, R-CNMI, said she supports securing U.S. borders and stopping anyone who "tries to abuse America’s immigration laws."

"If someone is exploiting our immigration system, they should be prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law. I have zero tolerance for fraud or abuse," King-Hinds said.

However, she claimed Griffith’s bill is targeting the wrong people – noting the 2009 shift in territorial immigration authority to the federal government.

King-Hinds said that with people admitted to CNMI under federal oversight, Congress should strengthen enforcement rather than "single-out" Americans living there.

King-Hinds said CNMI has a unique relationship with the mainland through what is called its "Covenant" – a negotiated agreement she said deserves "careful consideration" before Congress adjusts rights granted to people born there.

"I am also deeply concerned about creating one standard of citizenship for Americans born in the fifty states and another for Americans born in the territories. The people of the CNMI have disproportionately and proudly served in our Armed Forces, contributed to this nation, and lived as loyal Americans for generations. They should not become the testing ground for policies that Congress cannot or will not apply elsewhere."

"I stand ready to work with my colleagues to accomplish those goals, but I will always fight to protect the rights of the people of the Northern Mariana Islands," she said.

Fox News Digital also reached out to Dels. Stacey Plaskett, D-V.I., Pablo Jose Fernandez, D-P.R., and James Moylan, R-Guam, for comment.

In the 2022 case United States v. Vaello Madero, the Supreme Court upheld Congress’ exclusion of Puerto Rico residents from the Supplemental Security Income program. Justice Neil Gorsuch agreed with the judgment but wrote separately to argue that the Insular Cases should be overruled.

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

"A century ago in the Insular Cases, this court held that the federal government could rule Puerto Rico and other territories largely without regard to the Constitution. It is past time to acknowledge the gravity of this error and admit what we know to be true," Gorsuch wrote – potentially setting up another court battle, should Griffith’s bill gain further steam.

Other critics have countered that if territorial residents want full rights, they should actively pursue statehood as Alaska and Hawaii did in the modern era.

Griffith’s proposal could force Congress and the courts to confront how much authority lawmakers retain over citizenship rules in the territories.

Fox News Digital's Robert Schmad contributed to this report.

item.title: Bill Maher says it’s 'so stupid' for anti-Trump artists to bail on Kennedy Center performances
item.description: Bill Maher calls it "stupid" for artists to cancel Kennedy Center performances over President Donald Trump, saying that he doesn't own comedy.
item.content:encoded:
Comedian and talk show host Bill Maher said it’s "stupid" for musicians and artists to cancel Kennedy Center performances over President Donald Trump’s attempt to put his name on the iconic building. 

Maher recently accepted the Mark Twain Prize for American Humor at the Kennedy Center, where Trump serves as the chairman. The HBO comedian sat down with ABC’s Jonathan Karl, who asked about artists who have refused to perform at the Kennedy Center over Trump.

"That’s so stupid. First of all, you’re just giving him the victory. He doesn’t own the Kennedy Center. He doesn’t own comedy," Maher said in an extended version of the interview posted online. 

"Life goes on. That’s the message I would send," he continued. "I’ve always watched the Kennedy Center honors. This is kind of like that but for one guy. It’s the same thing. You’re up in the box, you know, it’s awesome. It’s great, and it’s rare, and it’s America, and he doesn’t own that, either." 

BILL MAHER ADMITS HE RESPECTS TRUMP FOR SEEING HIM AS RIVAL, TRYING TO BLOCK HIS AWARD

The musical "Hamilton," actress Issa Rae, banjo player Béla Fleck, the San Francisco Ballet, cellist Seth Parker Woods, violinist Hilary Hahn, composer Philip Glass, Renée Fleming, Vocal Arts DC, the Brentano Quartet, the Seattle Children's Theatre, the Martha Graham Dance Company, the Washington National Opera and Sonia De Los Santos are among the shows, performers and artists that have bailed on the Kennedy Center because of Trump, according to NPR. 

Maher dismissed people letting Trump "ruin everything," and made light of the Trump administration initially attempting to stop him from receiving the award.

"I was like, ‘Game on,'" Maher said. 

"This was after our dinner, our famous dinner, you know, back to sniping and yelling," he continued. "But, better than no dialogue, in my view, so, when he did that, I was like, ‘Good move, dude.’" 

JONATHAN TURLEY: TRUMP’S KENNEDY CENTER NAME CHANGE WILL KEEP LAWYERS BUSY FOR YEARS

Maher and Trump have traded barbs over the last year despite meeting for dinner at the White House last year, which landed the HBO host in hot water with some liberals. 

Maher, a longtime critic of Trump, said the president was "was gracious and measured" during the reportedly cordial meeting. But the White House dinner wasn’t enough to stop Trump from criticizing Maher, who previously suggested he respected the hostility from the administration. 

"I love that, you know, he takes the game seriously. Like no, ‘I’m going to block this guy.’ I respect the move," Maher said on his "Club Random" podcast in late April. 

 Maher has also said anyone who stopped watching his show because he had dinner with Trump is an "idiot." 

The battle over the Kennedy Center began in February 2025, shortly after Trump was inaugurated for a second time as president. He replaced several trustees on the board, while also appointing himself a trustee.

FEDERAL JUDGE ORDERS TRUMP'S NAME REMOVED FROM KENNEDY CENTER, SAYS ONLY CONGRESS CAN RENAME IT

The new Trump-allied board then elected him chair, enabling him to fire the previous president of the center, Deborah Rutter. 

Trump and the center’s board changed the name of the Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts to "Trump-Kennedy Center," but U.S. District Judge Christopher Cooper, an Obama appointee, said the iconic venue cannot be renamed without an act of Congress.

The saga culminated on June 13, when workers began removing Trump's name from the Kennedy Center. The sign previously said "The Donald J. Trump and John F. Kennedy Memorial Center For The Performing Arts."

Fox News Digital’s Hanna Panreck, James Cirrone and Lindsay Kornick contributed to this report.

item.title: Family identifies young woman found fatally shot in home owned by Rep Ayanna Pressley's husband
item.description: Family members identified the young woman found fatally shot inside a Boston property owned by Rep. Ayanna Pressley's husband as police continue investigating.
item.content:encoded:
Family has identified the young woman found fatally shot Saturday inside a Boston home owned by the husband of Rep. Ayanna Pressley, D-Mass.

While police have not officially identified the victim, her family identified her as 18-year-old Yoseliani Marte in a GoFundMe campaign created by her mother.

In the fundraiser, Marte's mother said her daughter was killed as a "result of domestic violence."

Police responded to a residence in Boston's Mattapan neighborhood around 1:52 p.m. Saturday, where they found the victim suffering from a single gunshot wound, according to police reports obtained by Fox News Digital.

BOSTON POLICE INVESTIGATE DEATH AT HOME OWNED BY REP AYANNA PRESSLEY'S HUSBAND

According to public records, the home is owned by Conan Harris, who has been married to Pressley since 2014.

Pressley and Harris do not live at the two-family residence, which has been reported as a rental property, according to the Boston Herald.

COMPANY OF 'SQUAD' MEMBER'S HUSBAND LANDS $2B CONTRACT FROM BLUE STATE FOR NEW COURTHOUSE

The home is currently listed for sale for $1.15 million.

A spokesperson for Pressley's office told Boston 25 News that neither the congresswoman nor her family were involved in the incident and that Pressley extends her deepest condolences to the family and loved ones impacted by the tragedy.

FITNESS TRAINER TOLD FRIEND SHE WAS 'SCARED FOR HER LIFE' WEEKS BEFORE BODY WITH MATCHING CLOTHING FOUND

A neighbor said the usually calm neighborhood was shaken by the large police response Saturday.

"We got home and there were police coming down the street, they blocked off with crime tape over there and neighbors gathered around," a neighbor said, according to Boston 25 News.

"I mean it’s sad, it’s really sad because you know this neighborhood is a wonderful place to be."

Family and friends reportedly held a vigil for the victim Sunday night.

The manner of death remains under investigation, Boston police said.

Fox News Digital has reached out to Pressley’s office for comment.

item.title: 'Game of Thrones' star Lena Headey says outrage over infamous nude scene left her stunned
item.description: Lena Headey reveals why she used a body double for Cersei's "Walk of Atonement" and reflects on the predatory power dynamics young actresses face.
item.content:encoded:
More than 10 years after one of television's most talked-about scenes, Lena Headey set the record straight about a decision that sparked backlash far beyond Westeros.

Headey pulled back the curtain on the backlash that erupted after Cersei Lannister's infamous "Walk of Atonement" in "Game of Thrones," revealing she still found the outrage baffling.

"I was really shocked by the anger, by this idea that I’d duped the audience," Headey told The Telegraph. "But by that point everyone knew [the cast], it was insane simply going anywhere, and I was with 3,000 extras."

'OFF CAMPUS' STAR ELLA BRIGHT ADDRESSES AGE CONCERNS OVER INTIMATE SCENES IN PROVOCATIVE SERIES

She explained that while acting is "a joy," the scale of filming the scene would have made it impossible for her to fully inhabit the emotional performance if she had also been worrying about being naked in front of thousands of people.

"I wouldn’t have been able to do the emotional part of the job," she told the outlet. "I’d have been in full-on defensive mode."

Looking back, Headey said she was in a very different position than many younger actresses entering the industry. Her experience on set gave her the confidence to set boundaries she doesn't believe everyone feels comfortable asserting.

GOLDIE HAWN SOUNDS THE ALARM ON CHILDHOOD CELEBRITY CULTURE: 'ALL I CAN SAY IS IT'S A NIGHTMARE'

"By that point, I’d been through the wringer," the actress said. "I could stand up for myself. I’m not saying those actresses couldn’t, but they were much younger and more vulnerable, and had far less experience of being in front of people and the camera and performing. Whereas I was of an age where I’d just tit about and diffuse anything uncomfortable with idiocy."

At the time, Headey told Entertainment Weekly some viewers believed she was "less of an actress" because she didn't perform the nudity herself.

"I've done nudity. I'm not averse to it," she explained in 2016. "But I know I'm a very emotional actor and I get really driven by that. In order to do my job, I allow myself to be really vulnerable. I don't know any other way to do my job. Things really affect me. And the thought of being naked for three days and trying to contain her in the way she would be I think I would feel very angry. I didn't want to be angry. I don't think Cersei would be angry. I did what I thought she would do, emotionally."

Headey praised body double Rebecca Van Cleave for taking on the physically demanding role, acknowledging that appearing nude on camera was far from easy. She also said being a mother and an established public figure made filming the nude portions herself feel like more than she was willing to take on.

"I didn't phone it in," she noted of her performance. "I was actually there for three days with Rebecca."

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

For Headey, the controversy also reflected a much bigger issue she believes many actresses faced early in their careers.

"There was this rite of passage all young female actors had to go through, which usually involved snogging and falling in love, and having sex and showing your boobs," she told The Telegraph. "They’d call them the ingénue parts, to make it sound nicer."

The "300" actress admitted she rarely questioned those expectations when she was starting out because she was simply grateful to be working.

"But I just got on with it," she explained. "I didn’t go to drama school, so I would just arrive on a set and be, ‘Oh my God, I’ve got a job.’ And when it came to those moments, I don’t think I even questioned that I should be safe. Instead, I’d go home and cry, or think, ‘Oh, that felt weird and too familiar.’ Now I look back and feel, ‘Hmm, that was rough.’"

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Those experiences ultimately changed the way Headey viewed power dynamics throughout the entertainment industry.

"The weird protection that we offer predatory men in the business because of the disproportionate power they wield, set against the need among vulnerable actresses to work to put food on the table to get the job – it makes me very angry. A job can be completely soured by one person who, for some reason, is allowed to get away with it."

She said the #MeToo movement exposed how widespread those experiences were and believes younger actresses today are far more willing to reject situations that make them uncomfortable.

"The attitude today is, ‘I’m not f---ing doing that,'" she emphasized.

item.title: Sophie Cunningham acted like an adult this weekend while Angel Reese pandered, red-hot Red Sox & Cubs SMACK!
item.description: Angel Reese's "protected species" controversy drew a WNBA suspension while Sophie Cunningham spent her weekend acting like a normal adult person.
item.content:encoded:
'Nightcaps' is a daily column that catches you up on the biggest sports, news and culture stories you may have missed while pretending to do work at your desk. It runs Monday–Friday on OutKick.com.

Whoaaaaaaaaaaa Nellie! What a weekend. What a weird, exhausting, annoying, weekend full of insufferable WNBA antics that make me truly question whether or not we're all just living in a simulation.

Goodness. Thank God SEC Media Days start today, and NFL teams start reporting to camp this week. That means football is close.

ANGEL REESE’S LATEST WNBA CONTROVERSY SHOWS HOW VICTIMHOOD BECAME CENTRAL TO HER BRAND

The World Cup is over, which means we finally get new episodes of Masterchef again. It also means we're one step closer to returning to real football in this country.

More of that, please, and less of Angel Reese. PLEASE.

We'll dive in here in a bit.

Welcome to a Monday Nightcaps — the one where Sophie Cunningham acts like an adult while the rest of the WNBA panders to the #MOB for the umpteenth time. It's so painfully obvious how silly this league is after this weekend, as if it wasn't before.

What else? I've got Ryan Fox winning the British Open in record time, Rory being full of bologna, and my Red Sox refusing to lose a game. Seriously, have y'all seen this run they're on right now? It's insane.

Penultimate Monday of July, folks. Dial it in. Fall is inching closer. Time to start getting those affairs in order for Week 1.

Start working on those summer chores NOW. Get the lawn fixed. Get the garage cleaned out. Check off all the boxes in July, so you're ready to roll in August.

But first, enjoy a Monday 'Cap:

I've covered a lot of silly things out of the WNBA since it became semi-relevant a few years back, but this Angel Reese nonsense I had to write about — twice! — on Saturday is now the leader in the clubhouse.

Imagine being as insane as these people are. You just wake up every day, looking for the next "racist" comment so you can virtue-signal and play the victim. I knew the second I saw the Tempo coach call Angel Reese a "protected species," it was going to blow up.

And then Angel reposted it and dangerously spread a false narrative.

And then the Tempo coach predictably apologized to the MOB, for literally no reason. None at all.

And then, right on cue, the WNBA suspended her for one game. Why? I have no earthly idea. The girl who choked Caitlin Clark got the same suspension a coach did for calling a player a "protected species." Think about that.

They're screaming 'RACISM!' because a coach argued that a player was getting special treatment — which has literally been happening for as long as sports have existed.

Think about how silly these people are, and how silly this league is. It's an unserious league.

Hell, it's not a league. It's a movement. That's what the WNBA is. If we start treating it like a movement, and not a sports league, it all starts to make so much sense.

"Zach, just stop writing about it!"

That's the obvious response, and one I will undoubtedly get later today. I can't do that. If we do that, they win. And they may still win, but at least we'll be able to stand in their way a little longer.

Anyway, I say all that to highlight Sophie Cunningham, who is, easily, the most marketable, most sane person in that insufferable league.

While Angel was spreading dangerous lies on the internet and showing off a level of ignorance that I didn't know existed in a person, Sophie spent her weekend hosting a camp for 300 kids and acting like an adult.

Choose your fighter, America:

If the WNBA wants to market their league and actually grow the game, Sophie Cunningham should be front and center.

That's right. I said it. Sophie Cunningham is the most marketable person in the league right now, and it's not particularly close. She has fun. She doesn't take the game too seriously. She doesn't race-bait or pander or virtue-signal.

She spent her Sunday at a camp playing basketball against 300 kids. They all wanted to do the "point" with her. She wasn't online race-baiting or pandering or acting silly.

What a concept!

She doesn't do the nonsense, mainly because she's a normal person. There's an idea Just be a normal person. It goes a long way!

Let's see if the WNBA takes or leaves that free advice ...

"The WNBA Players Association is currently prohibiting licensees from using the Sophie point, saying that it is "derogatory."

Allllllllllrighty then! Morons.

Good luck to that league. Football season starts in a month.

Thank goodness.

OK, let's move on because we're 700 words in on a Monday and I'm quite sure I've filled my WNBA quota for the day.

Anyone keeping up with what the Boston Red Sox are doing right now in Major League Baseball? Thirteen wins in a row. 18-2 in their last 20. From 6 games out of a wild card spot to 1.5 games up. Only six out in the division.

Incredible:

I buried the Sox earlier this year, rightfully so. The team STUNK. Naturally, Red Sox fan Bill made sure to keep me on my toes over the weekend:

The Tracy Turnaround continues. Who said the talent from Triple A WOOSOX would be called up to turn the RedSox around? Oh yes, me!

The 2nd place YankeesSuck have been swept, now the 1st place Tiny Rays look to be next after losing 4 straight already.

Zach, your analysis has been poor and your commentary on the Sox worse.

Thanks, Bill! To be fair, my analysis was spot-on up until two weeks ago. I did not see an 18-2 run coming, no. Hand up, I'll be the first to admit that.

If Red Sox fan Bill truly saw it coming, I'd like him to email me tonight with the lotto numbers. If it wins, I'll split it 50/50!

OK, rapid-fire time on this Monday. First up? How about the balls on Rory McIlroy calling out Bryson for being "performative" during Friday's second round?

Well ain't that the pot calling the kettle black:

Yeah, come on. What are we doing here? Rory hates Bryson, and that's fine. I like it, actually. Golf needs a good rivalry. It's great for the game.

But don't sit up there and call out Bryson DeChambeau when you bitch and moan as much as anyone on tour. Let's be realistic, Rory. For once.

You know who's NOT performative? British Open champ Ryan Fox, who is, without a doubt, the fastest golfer I've ever seen play:

This Ryan Fox is incredible. Admittedly, I'd barely heard of the guy until this weekend. But watching him over the final few holes yesterday — specifically the last two shots — was just a joy. Guy just got up there and let it rip. No thinking. No lining up. No mulling it over with his caddie. No standing over a 3-foot putt for an hour.

Give me a club and let's get on with it. We could all learn something from that, myself included.

OK, that's it for today. Good start to the week, everyone. We're in the stretch run of July. Let's keep grinding and end this month strong.

And if you're going to a baseball game tonight — especially in Chicago — stand your butt UP during our great national anthem!

Is the Angel Reese story the dumbest thing you've seen today? Email me at Zach.Dean@OutKick.com.

item.title: Cruise ship hit with third stomach illness outbreak in two months, review underway
item.description: Nearly one-third of passengers aboard the National Geographic Sea Bird cruise ship reported vomiting and "abdominal cramps" in the vessel's third outbreak since May.
item.content:encoded:
Health officials have reported a gastrointestinal illness outbreak aboard an Alaska cruise ship, marking the vessel's third stomach bug outbreak in about two months.

The National Geographic Sea Bird, operated by Lindblad Expeditions, was the subject of a July 15 report by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's Vessel Sanitation Program (VSP). 

The CDC said the outbreak aboard the trip was reported July 12 — halfway through its July 10 to July 15 voyage from Sitka to Juneau, Alaska.

STOMACH BUGS SURGING AS DOCTORS SAY CERTAIN TRAVEL ESSENTIALS COULD SAVE YOUR TRIP

The latest incident marks the third gastrointestinal illness outbreak reported aboard the ship since late May, according to the VSP.

In the most recent outbreak, 18 of the ship's 62 passengers — or nearly a third of those aboard — reported being ill.

The predominant symptoms were "vomiting [and] abdominal cramps," the report said. As of Monday, the source of the outbreak is unknown. 

Officials reported that the ship's crew isolated the ill passengers and crew. Officials also increased "cleaning and disinfection procedures according to their outbreak prevention and response plan."

CRUISE TIPPING CHARGES ARE 'OUT OF CONTROL,' SAY PASSENGERS AS COMPANIES TOUT 'EXCEPTIONAL SERVICE'

"VSP is remotely monitoring the situation, including review of the ship's outbreak response and sanitation procedures," the statement added.

The CDC said the cause of the outbreak remains unknown, though norovirus is a common cause of gastrointestinal illness outbreaks on cruise ships.

"Finding the agent that caused an outbreak [meaning the causative agent] can take time," said the VSP. "When an outbreak occurs, people whose symptoms [meet] the case definition are asked to provide stool or vomitus samples."

BELOVED CRUISE SPOT WILL BE OFF LIMITS THROUGH 2027 AS SAFETY CONCERNS LINGER

"In this outbreak, samples are pending confirmatory testing," the report also noted.

Fox News Digital reached out to Lindblad Expeditions and National Geographic Expeditions, which did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Fox News Digital also reached out to the CDC.

Users on an online forum expressed disgust over the latest outbreak.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

"They need to use bleach to sanitize every inch of that ship," one commenter said.

"Gross," another person said.

After a previous outbreak on the National Geographic Sea Bird two months ago, a Reddit user wrote, "It happens every year and all the time."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE LIFESTYLE NEWS

Another wrote at the time, "My sister and I were on the Sea Bird, on the cruise [that] left Sitka on May 31st. Lindblad told us they were doing extra sanitizing of the ship."

The incident follows a string of cruise-related health incidents that have made headlines in recent months.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

In April, a hantavirus outbreak aboard the MV Hondius drew international attention after it was linked to three passenger deaths.

Also in the spring, more than 100 people fell ill during a norovirus outbreak aboard the Caribbean Princess, according to the CDC.

The National Geographic Sea Bird is a 152-foot expedition vessel carrying up to 62 guests. 

Its shallow draft allows access to narrow inlets and wildlife-rich fjords beyond the reach of larger ships.

item.title: Hailey Ostrom is back defending summer at the lake armed with her golf club and bikini
item.description: Hailey Ostrom crushed golf balls off moonscape in a bikini, delivering a reminder that summer isn't over despite early Halloween decorations.
item.content:encoded:
Believe it or not, the fight against the creep of fall into summer has already begun. The disrespect is at an all-time high. It really hit home for me last July when a Halloween display was out at a grocery store at the beach.

The beach? They're making their money off of summer vacations in July. What are they doing? Our very own Senior Editor of Content Development has already started receiving reports of Halloween decorations in the wild.

My own wife has fallen victim to it. This weekend, while her parents were in town and the temperature was well into the 90s, she came home with a Halloween-themed bag. But all hope is not lost.

POPULAR GOLF INFLUENCER REVEALS WILD STORY OF FAN WHO GOT HER AUTOGRAPH TATTOOED ON HIS ARM

That's where golf influencer Hailey Ostrom comes in. I know she was in Sunday Screencaps taking on Lake Powell, but she stepped up again for summer on Monday, still out at the lake.

Her message over the weekend on Twitter was slightly different from the one she delivered on Instagram. This message was straight to the point, "Just here to remind you it’s summertime."

How do you do that? You do that out on a boat in a bikini enjoying the water. You also do that by dusting off a trusty play from the content playbook. That's what Hailey did on Monday.

JENA SIMS ATTEMPS TO SPLIT THE 'G' BEFORE LEAVING ENGLAND, RAY LEWIS TRAINS IN MMA & MY WAR WITH VERIZON

She pulled out her driver, as she's done before, and she crushed golf balls off the moonscape all in the name of respecting summer.

That is how you fight the creep of fall. Listen, fall is great, mostly because it brings with it football, but let's keep the Halloween decorations in the warehouse until fall arrives.

We do that by not purchasing the early arriving fall items and shaming those around us who do. We also fight the creep by smashing golf balls off of moonscape in a bikini.

Let's take a moment as we wrap this up to recognize and thank Hailey Ostrom for her continued support of and respect for summer. We're only gifted a limited number of them in our lifetimes.

item.title: Inside underground El Paso storm drain system Border Patrol says cartels exploit to smuggle illegal migrants
item.description: Border Patrol says 32 underground entry points from Mexico lead to exits in elementary schools, parks and backyards across El Paso neighborhoods.
item.content:encoded:
Hidden beneath the streets of El Paso lies what U.S. Border Patrol agents describe as "a city underneath our city," a sprawling network of storm drains built for flood control that authorities say Mexican cartels continue exploiting to smuggle illegal migrants from Mexico into the United States.

Fox News was granted rare, exclusive access underground with the El Paso Sector’s elite Confined Space Entry Team (CSET), joining agents as they navigated the dangerous maze of tunnels where Border Patrol says smugglers continue searching for vulnerabilities, despite illegal crossings and apprehensions at historically low levels.

Border Patrol told Fox News it takes smugglers and illegal migrants 45 minutes to an hour to make it through the storm drains, which are hot and tight, with oxygen levels that can change or drop unexpectedly.

The underground system stretches for miles beneath El Paso. According to Border Patrol, there are 32 known entry points from the Rio Grande into the United States and hundreds of exit points scattered throughout neighborhoods, parks and commercial areas across the city.

WATCH: INSIDE LOOK AT THE DANGEROUS CARTEL HUMAN SMUGGLING TUNNELS STILL BEING USED AT BORDER

Jason Hartman, who leads the El Paso Sector’s Confined Space Entry Team, said one of the biggest challenges is where smugglers can emerge once they make it through the underground network.

"There are exit points into schools, into elementary schools, where we sleep, where we play," Hartman told Fox News. "At any point, you can have smugglers or bad guys coming up in your backyard, in your playgrounds, in your kids’ schools."

Just outside one of the storm drain entrances, evidence of previous crossings remained behind. Fox News observed tools, a wallet and even a Mexican driver’s license left near the entrance, items agents say migrants and smugglers often abandon during the journey.

MEXICAN NATIONAL SENTENCED IN BORDER CHILD SMUGGLING CASE INVOLVING THC-LACED CANDY

Border Patrol guards entry points near the Rio Grande, and Hartman says agents rely on technology to detect movement inside the underground network.

"We do have technology that lets us know when they’re in there, where they’re going, when they come out," Hartman said. "Technology grows at a rapid pace."

Still, some manage to slip through. So far this fiscal year, Border Patrol says agents have apprehended 161 illegal migrants that used El Paso’s storm drain system. At the height of the border crisis, Hartman said agents sometimes encountered groups of up to 70 migrants moving through the underground maze. Today, he said, it’s more common to encounter groups of just two or three.

SIX ILLEGAL IMMIGRANTS, INCLUDING 14-YEAR-OLD, FOUND DEAD IN SHIPPING CONTAINER ALONG SOUTHERN BORDER

Agents assigned to the specialized team undergo extensive training in a full-scale replica storm drain, learning how to operate in confined spaces while constantly monitoring oxygen levels that can change without warning.

Hartman said the physical conditions alone can be exhausting before agents ever encounter a smuggler.

"For me, it’s the heat and the time and the air," Hartman said.

Border Patrol says because it is more difficult and risky to illegally cross the border, smugglers have dramatically increased what they charge migrants, between $20,000 and $30,000 per person, to guide them through the storm drains.

"Cartels are desperate to try to get people in," said Hamid Nikseresht, a U.S. Border Patrol Special Operations supervisor. "They’re desperate to try to fund the networks globally that they fund because this isn’t just a localized issue. This is a global issue."

item.title: 'Today' suspect caught on camera before hate crime arrest, rap sheet reveals attempted kidnapping conviction
item.description: Andrew Truelove's criminal record spans two decades, including attempted kidnapping and making threats, before his alleged hate crime involving Craig Melvin.
item.content:encoded:
The man accused of a hate crime when he approached "Today" co-host Craig Melvin at NBC's Manhattan studio has a criminal record spanning at least two decades, with charges including attempted kidnapping and trespassing, according to court records.

New York City police arrested Andrew Truelove, 41, last week after NBC says he snuck into a vestibule near Studio 1A in Rockefeller Center and approached Melvin.

A review of footage from the show reveals a man resembling Truelove and wearing similar clothing was pictured in the background as early as 8:03 a.m. the day of the incident. At 8:05 a.m., the man in the background took off a backpack and appeared to place it in or behind a bush outside the building. He could be seen multiple times until 8:34 a.m., when actress Anne Hathaway was on set to discuss her role in the latest adaption of "The Odyssey."

'TODAY' SHOW INTRUDER IDENTIFIED AFTER ALLEGEDLY BREACHING SECURITY NEAR CRAIG MELVIN

According to the NYPD, Truelove was arrested at 8:57 a.m. after he allegedly confronted Melvin with a racial slur in a private area of the building not open to the public.

He was allegedly looking for Al Roker when he found Melvin instead.

In October 2007, Truelove was accused of trying to abduct an 8-year-old from a school in Norfolk, Virginia. He was convicted at trial and sentenced to 10 years in prison, of which six were suspended, followed by 10 years on probation, court records show.

GO HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE FROM THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM

A psychiatrist testified he had symptoms of bipolar disorder and had stopped taking medications after gaining more than 100 pounds prior to the incident, according to The Virginian-Pilot.

Truelove has an extensive rap sheet in the state of Virginia, where court records show he was also accused of trespassing, destruction of property, making bomb threats and multiple probation violations.

In 2023, he served 90 days in jail for the property destruction case. In 2014, he was arrested on charges of stealing signs from memorials dedicated to the Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting victims.

PIMA COUNTY SHERIFF ISSUES ALERT FOR KIDNAPPING SUSPECT LESS THAN 10 MILES FROM NANCY GUTHRIE'S HOME

He faces hate crime charges of burglary, a felony, and menacing, a misdemeanor, in connection with the Melvin incident.

FOLLOW THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM ON X

"An individual entered an unauthorized area in a vestibule near Studio 1A," a "Today" spokesperson said in a statement. "The person approached anchor Craig Melvin, who alerted security. The individual was detained and taken into custody by law enforcement without further incident. There was no altercation, and no one was injured. TODAY is cooperating fully with law enforcement as they investigate the matter. NBC and TODAY take the safety and security of our employees, talent, staff and guests extremely seriously. We are reviewing the incident and our security protocols and remain committed to providing a safe and secure environment for everyone who works at and visits our studios."

Court records in New York show man with the same name and age as Truelove has additional pending cases in connection with an alleged assault in the NYPD's 5th Precinct in January, trespassing and reckless endangerment charges on the city's transit system from May — and a 12-count criminal mischief indictment from last year.

He's now being held on $10,000 bail in a New York City jail.

Melvin's colleague, Savannah Guthrie, is the daughter of missing Nancy Guthrie, whose Feb. 1 disappearance remains unsolved. She is believed to have been abducted from her home after Nest video shows a masked intruder on her front steps in the Catalina Foothills of northern Tucson, Arizona.

GOT A TIP?

Anyone with information on that case is asked to call 1-800-FBI or 520-88-CRIME.

With no arrests or publicly identified suspects in the Guthrie case, some web sleuths have pondered a potential connection. However, Truelove has not been linked to the abduction by any law enforcement agency, and he does not match the physical description of a masked man seen on Guthrie's front porch around the time she was taken.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

New York jail records list Truelove as about 6 feet tall and 250 pounds. The masked intruder has been described by the FBI as 5 feet, 9 inches to 5 feet, 10 inches tall with an average build.

The Pima County Sheriff's Department and FBI did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

GET BREAKING NEWS BY EMAIL

Truelove has reportedly traveled through the Sonoran Desert region, according to a 2023 profile in the Los Angeles Times that focused on his bout with homelessness on the West Coast after getting off of probation in Virginia.

It was not immediately clear when he arrived in New York City.

Truelove is due back in court Wednesday.

item.title: Sen Darline Graham reveals whether she will run for a full-term during ‘Hannity’ exclusive
item.description: Darline Graham, who was sworn into office earlier this month following the sudden death of her brother, appears on Fox News Channel's 'Hannity.'
item.content:encoded:
Sen. Darline Graham, R-S.C., the sister of late Sen. Lindsey Graham, told Fox News Channel’s Sean Hannity on Monday that she plans for a full Senate term. 

Darline Graham was sworn into office last week following the sudden death of her brother, becoming the first female senator from the Palmetto State and the first sister of a lawmaker ever appointed to the upper chamber. She sat down with "Hannity" for her first interview since her brother's tragic passing and her appointment to the U.S. Senate. 

Hannity noted that South Carolina law dictates that she will need to decide if she plans to run for a full term before her brother’s funeral has even taken place, as the filing period for a special primary runs from July 21 to July 28.

"It is a tremendous amount of pressure," Graham told Hannity.

TUNE IN TO WATCH THE FULL INTERVIEW ON 'HANNITY' TONIGHT AT 9 PM ET ON FOX NEWS CHANNEL!

"I have been praying a lot, talking to my family. But with the love of my family, with the support of his staff, I know I’m a hard worker. I learned that from Lindsey," she continued. "He cared about the people of this state. I have worked for almost 30 years for the people of this state." 

Graham then said, "I feel like I can do it. I feel an inner peace about it."

Hannity then pressed Graham on whether she has decided. 

"I’m in," she said. 

The late Lindsey Graham’s communications director said Friday that the senator's "life and legacy" will be celebrated on Tuesday, July 28, in Washington, D.C., and on Wednesday, July 29, in Columbia and Pickens County, South Carolina.

LINDSEY GRAHAM'S FINAL ACT REVERBERATES IN SENATE AS SISTER IS URGED TO 'KEEP PEDALING'

Lindsey Graham died July 11 at age 71.

A statement released by Graham's office on Sunday cited preliminary findings from the District of Columbia's Office of the Chief Medical Examiner, identifying the cause of death as an aortic dissection due to arteriosclerotic cardiovascular disease.

The full interview with Sen. Darline Graham airs tonight on "Hannity" at 9 p.m. ET. 

Fox News Digital’s Greg Norman contributed to this report.

item.title: Border czar Tom Homan fires back at Margaret Brennan over ICE pursuit
item.description: Tom Homan clashed with CBS' Margaret Brennan over whether ICE officers activated emergency lights before fatally shooting Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Houston.
item.content:encoded:
White House border czar Tom Homan defended U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement's (ICE) use of unmarked vehicles Sunday on CBS' "Face the Nation," clashing with moderator Margaret Brennan over whether officers displayed emergency lights while pursuing a Houston man who was fatally shot.

Homan argued that unmarked vehicles helped officers locate suspects and maintained that the recent deaths could have been avoided through compliance.

"But bottom line is, we’ll see when the investigation plays out, but they’d be alive today if they simply complied with a vehicle stop by federal law enforcement officer. Just comply," Homan said.

ICE SAYS OFFICER SHOT AND KILLED ILLEGAL IMMIGRANT WHO TRIED TO RAM HIM WITH CAR IN HOUSTON

Brennan raised concerns about the unmarked vehicle used before an ICE officer fatally shot 52-year-old Lorenzo Salgado Araujo. Salgado’s family said he believed he was being pursued by robbers rather than federal agents.

"Well, the family of the man in Houston said that he thought he was being robbed because it was an unmarked vehicle," Brennan said. "Not that it was law enforcement at all."

Homan maintained that ICE vehicles contain equipment allowing officers to identify themselves during stops.

"They had emergency lights. They activate emergency lights. Blue lights," Homan said. "You know, in my vehicle I drive, where I’m with my security detail, we have emergency lights."

ICE AGENTS IN FATAL HOUSTON SHOOTING WERE NOT WEARING BODY CAMERAS, SOURCES SAY

Brennan challenged Homan to substantiate that claim, noting that publicly available videos did not appear to show emergency lights and the officers involved were not wearing body cameras.

"That there were lights, because the videos that we’ve seen there aren’t, and because there weren’t body cameras," Brennan said. "It is hard to substantiate some of what you are saying."

Homan acknowledged that he had not reviewed the videos of the Houston incident. 

"OK, well, I have not seen the videos," Homan said.

The border czar qualified his earlier claim, saying the vehicles should have contained lights and sirens but acknowledging that he could not confirm whether officers used them.

"Now, if they didn’t activate the lights, again, I haven’t seen that video," Homan said. "If they didn’t activate the lights, then there’s an issue there, right?"

"A lot of police departments, I’m in New York City right now, a lot of NYPD officers are driving unmarked vehicles, and that helps them find people that don’t want to be found, right?"

ICE RESUMES NATIONWIDE TRAFFIC STOPS UNDER NEW POLICY REQUIRING BODY CAMERAS

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE
